beats.text: An estimator is a recipe that turns random data into a guess about a fixed population parameter.
Two numbers grade an estimator: bias is how far its average lands from the truth, variance is how much it bounces.

visual.note: Mean squared error splits into variance plus bias-squared.

beats.text: Sample variance divides by n minus one because deviations are measured from the fitted mean, which sits too close.

visual.note: Bessel's correction makes the sample variance exactly unbiased.

beats.text: An estimator is consistent if it converges to the truth as the sample grows, since the mean's variance shrinks as one over n.
Maximum likelihood picks the parameter value that makes the observed data most probable by maximizing the log-likelihood.

visual.note: MLEs are consistent and asymptotically efficient.

beats.text: Averaging ten draws from a population of variance twenty-five cuts the error variance to two point five.

visual.label: MSE of the mean, sigma-squared over n

beats.text: The sampling distribution is the distribution of an estimator across the many samples you could have drawn.
Its spread is the standard error, which shrinks as one over the square root of n.

visual.note: To halve your error you must quadruple your data.

beats.text: The Central Limit Theorem makes the standardized mean approach a standard normal whatever the parent shape.

visual.note: Holds for finite variance; fails for heavy tails like Cauchy.

beats.text: A ninety-five percent interval is the estimate plus or minus one point nine six standard errors.

visual.note: Use the t distribution when sigma is estimated.

beats.text: The ninety-five percent refers to the procedure, not to any single interval; repeated forever, that fraction would cover the truth.
Averaging a hundred measurements of spread ten gives a standard error of one.

concepts.title: Hypothesis testing: null, p-value, errors, power

beats.text: A hypothesis test states a boring null of no effect and asks how surprising the data would be if it were true.
The p-value is the probability, computed under the null, of a statistic at least as extreme as the one observed.

visual.note: Not the probability that the null is true.

beats.text: When the null is exactly true the p-value is uniform on zero to one, which is what makes a five percent threshold honest.
Type one error is a false alarm, type two is a miss, and power is the chance of catching a real effect.

visual.note: The four levers are locked together.

beats.text: Power rises with the true effect size and with the square root of n, and falls with noisier data.
A trial with a twenty percent type two error rate has eighty percent power, the conventional design target.

concepts.title: t-tests: comparing means when σ is unknown

beats.text: In practice you rarely know sigma, so you estimate it with S, and that noisy estimate fattens the tails.

visual.sub: Comparing means when sigma is unknown

beats.text: The one-sample t statistic has the shape of a z-score but uses the sample standard deviation in its denominator.

visual.note: n minus one degrees of freedom; heavier tails at small n.

beats.text: At small degrees of freedom the t distribution has heavier tails than the normal and converges to it as n grows.
Welch's two-sample t compares two independent groups and does not assume their variances are equal.

visual.note: The modern default; strictly safer than the pooled test.

beats.text: The t-test wants roughly normal, independent data and is robust to mild non-normality but not to dependence or outliers.
With a sample mean of fifty-two against fifty, an SD of eight and n of a hundred, the t statistic is two point five.
To compare three or more means, ANOVA replaces many pairwise t-tests with one omnibus test.
Total variation splits exactly into variation between the groups plus variation within them.

visual.note: Between is signal; within is noise.

beats.text: Dividing each sum of squares by its degrees of freedom gives mean squares, and their ratio is the F statistic.
F is a signal-to-noise ratio: under the null it sits near one, and a real difference between groups inflates it.
For exactly two groups F equals t squared, so ANOVA and the two-sample t-test agree.
Between sum of squares one twenty over two and within three hundred over twenty-seven give an F of five point four.
A five percent false-positive rate per test compounds ruthlessly once you run many tests.
Run twenty independent null tests and the chance of at least one spurious hit is about sixty-four percent.

visual.note: Across m tests the chance of some false positive grows toward one.

beats.text: The Bonferroni correction restores control by testing each hypothesis at alpha divided by m.
p-hacking weaponizes the same arithmetic: trying many variables and reporting only the winner manufactures significance.
Large replication efforts found a substantial share of published findings failed to reproduce, tracing back to underpowering and multiplicity.
Testing four hypotheses at a family-wise five percent means each is judged at the tighter threshold of zero point zero one two five.
